response:
The error you are encountering stems from a fundamental incompatibility between .NET 7 and the older WPF assemblies that rely on the full .NET Framework 4.0, which are not natively supported in the cross-platform .NET Core or .NET 5+ ecosystem. When your Windows build attempts to load the WPF project, it is likely referencing legacy assembly versions that do not exist in the modern .NET runtime environment, causing the system to fail when looking for the specific PresentationFramework version you specified.

To resolve this issue while maintaining your cross-platform architecture, you must ensure that your WPF project is explicitly configured as a .NET 7 WPF application rather than a .NET Framework 4.0 project. This involves modifying the project file to target the modern runtime, which provides its own implementation of WPF that is compatible with .NET 7 on Windows, thereby eliminating the need for the legacy assembly that is currently missing.

cousin projects in your solution that are intended for Windows should be set to target `net7.0-windows` in their SDK-style project files, ensuring they use the correct modern WPF libraries that are included with the .NET 7 SDK. By aligning the target framework of your UI project with the main application's .NET 7 runtime, the dependency resolution will work correctly on Windows without requiring any fallback to the older .NET Framework infrastructure.

Your conditional compilation strategy for Mac needs to be adjusted to exclude the WPF project entirely from the build process when targeting macOS, as WPF is not supported on that platform. You can achieve this by using MSBuild conditions or separate project files that only include the WPF dependencies when the `$(OS)` or `$(TargetFramework)` indicates a Windows environment, ensuring the Cocoa code path is used exclusively on Mac without triggering the missing assembly error.

Finally, verify that your main application project does not directly reference the WPF assembly as a standard NuGet package or project reference in a way that forces a load on macOS, but instead uses an abstraction layer or interface to interact with the platform-specific UI implementation. This architectural separation allows the application to remain truly cross-platform, loading the WPF window only on Windows and the Cocoa window only on Mac, while keeping the core logic unified under the .NET 7 framework.
